categories.name: Home Office
categories.description: Expenses for business use of home

categories.name: State & Local Taxes
categories.description: SALT deductions and state tax credits

categories.name: Interest & Debt
categories.description: Mortgage, student loan, and business interest

categories.name: Real Estate & Land
categories.description: Real estate, land use, and property-based tax strategies

items.name: Home Office Deduction (Simplified Method)
items.description: Deduct $5 per square foot of home used for business, up to 300 sq ft ($1,500 max).
items.conditions: Must use space exclusively and regularly for business. Cannot be an employee.

items.name: Home Office Deduction (Actual Expenses)
items.description: Deduct actual expenses proportional to business use percentage of home.
items.conditions: Must use space exclusively and regularly for business. Requires calculating business use percentage.

items.name: Internet Service (Business Portion)
items.description: Deduct the business-use percentage of home internet costs.
items.conditions: Must allocate based on actual business use percentage. Keep records of usage.

items.name: Home Utilities (Business Portion)
items.description: Deduct electricity, gas, water proportional to home office square footage.
items.conditions: Only available with actual expense method. Based on office-to-home area ratio.

items.name: Home Insurance (Business Portion)
items.description: Deduct homeowners or renters insurance proportional to office use.
items.conditions: Only with actual expense method. Pro-rated by business use percentage.

items.name: Home Repairs & Maintenance (Business Portion)
items.description: Deduct repairs and maintenance costs proportional to business use of home.
items.conditions: General home repairs pro-rated; repairs to office space only are 100% deductible.

items.name: Home Office Depreciation
items.description: Depreciate the business-use portion of your home over 39 years.
items.conditions: Only for homeowners using actual expense method. May trigger depreciation recapture on sale.

items.name: Home Office Phone Line
items.description: Deduct a dedicated business phone line or business percentage of shared line.
items.conditions: Dedicated second line is 100% deductible. Personal line requires business use allocation.

items.name: Mortgage Interest (Business Portion)
items.description: Deduct the business-use portion of mortgage interest via home office deduction.
items.conditions: Only with actual expense method. Remaining personal portion may still be itemized.

items.name: Property Tax (Business Portion)
items.description: Deduct the business-use portion of real estate taxes through home office.
items.conditions: Business portion is a Schedule C deduction; personal portion falls under SALT cap.

items.name: Security System (Business Portion)
items.description: Deduct business-use percentage of home security system costs.
items.conditions: Pro-rated by business use percentage. Include monitoring fees and equipment.

items.name: Standard Mileage Deduction
items.description: Deduct $0.70 per business mile driven in 2025.
items.conditions: Must use vehicle for business. Cannot also claim actual vehicle expenses. Keep mileage log.

items.name: Actual Vehicle Expenses
items.description: Deduct actual costs of operating a vehicle for business: gas, oil, repairs, insurance, registration.
items.conditions: Must choose between actual expenses and standard mileage. Track all receipts and business use %.

items.name: Vehicle Depreciation (MACRS)
items.description: Depreciate a business vehicle over 5 years using MACRS depreciation tables.
items.conditions: Only with actual expense method. Luxury vehicle limits apply. Listed property rules.

items.name: Parking Fees & Tolls
items.description: Deduct parking fees and tolls for business travel (not commuting).
items.conditions: Must be for business purposes. Commuting costs are never deductible.

items.name: Public Transit for Business
items.description: Deduct costs of public transportation used for business travel.
items.conditions: Must be for business travel, not regular commuting.

items.name: Rideshare/Taxi for Business
items.description: Deduct Uber, Lyft, and taxi fares for business travel.
items.conditions: Must be for business purposes. Keep receipts and document business reason.

items.name: Vehicle Lease Payments (Business Portion)
items.description: Deduct the business-use percentage of vehicle lease payments.
items.conditions: Inclusion amount may reduce deduction for expensive vehicles. Must use actual expense method.

items.name: Vehicle Insurance (Business Portion)
items.description: Deduct the business-use percentage of auto insurance premiums.
items.conditions: Only with actual expense method. Pro-rated by business mileage percentage.

items.name: Vehicle Registration & License (Business Portion)
items.description: Deduct business portion of annual registration and license fees.
items.conditions: Only with actual expense method. Based on business use percentage.

items.name: Heavy SUV/Truck (Over 6,000 lbs GVWR)
items.description: Vehicles over 6,000 lbs GVWR qualify for higher Section 179 limits up to $30,500 in 2025.
items.conditions: Vehicle must exceed 6,000 lbs GVWR. Must be used more than 50% for business.

items.name: Electric Vehicle Credit
items.description: Tax credit up to $7,500 for qualifying new electric vehicles purchased in 2025.
items.conditions: Vehicle must meet assembly, battery component, and critical mineral requirements. MAGI limits apply.

items.name: Laptop/Computer Purchase
items.description: Deduct the cost of a computer used for business purposes.
items.conditions: Must be used more than 50% for business. Can expense under Section 179 or depreciate.

items.name: Monitor/Display Purchase
items.description: Deduct cost of monitors and displays used for business.
items.conditions: Must be used primarily for business. Can expense or depreciate.

items.name: Desk & Office Furniture
items.description: Deduct cost of desks, chairs, and other office furniture for business use.
items.conditions: Must be used for business. Items over $2,500 should be capitalized and depreciated or expensed under §179.

items.name: Section 179 Expensing
items.description: Immediately expense qualifying business equipment up to $1,250,000 in 2025.
items.conditions: Phase-out begins at $3,130,000 in total equipment purchases. Must be placed in service during tax year.

items.name: Bonus Depreciation (60% in 2025)
items.description: First-year bonus depreciation of 60% for qualifying assets placed in service in 2025.
items.conditions: Applies to new and used property with recovery period of 20 years or less. Phase-down from 100% continues.

items.name: Smartphone (Business Use)
items.description: Deduct cost and monthly plan for a smartphone used for business.
items.conditions: Deduct only business-use percentage. Keep records of business vs personal use.

items.name: Server & Networking Equipment
items.description: Deduct servers, routers, switches, and networking hardware for business.
items.conditions: Can expense under Section 179 or depreciate over 5 years.

items.name: Camera & Video Equipment
items.description: Deduct cameras, lighting, and video equipment used for business content creation.
items.conditions: Must be used primarily for business. Listed property rules may apply.

items.name: Printer/Scanner
items.description: Deduct cost of printers, scanners, and multifunction devices for business.
items.conditions: Must be used for business. Items under de minimis threshold can be expensed immediately.

items.name: Tablet/iPad (Business Use)
items.description: Deduct cost of tablets used for business purposes.
items.conditions: Must be used more than 50% for business. Track business use percentage.

items.name: Accounting & Bookkeeping Fees
items.description: Deduct fees paid to accountants, bookkeepers, and CPA firms.
items.conditions: Business accounting fees are fully deductible on Schedule C. Personal tax prep fees are not deductible 2018-2025.

items.name: Legal Fees (Business)
items.description: Deduct attorney fees for business-related legal matters.
items.conditions: Must be ordinary and necessary business expense. Personal legal fees are generally not deductible.

items.name: Consulting Fees
items.description: Deduct fees paid to business consultants and advisors.
items.conditions: Must be ordinary and necessary. Issue 1099-NEC if payments exceed $600.

items.name: Tax Preparation Fees (Business)
items.description: Deduct costs of preparing business tax returns (Schedule C, partnership, S-corp).
items.conditions: Only business portion of tax prep is deductible. Personal return prep is not deductible through 2025.

items.name: Financial Planning Fees (Business)
items.description: Deduct fees for business financial planning and advisory services.
items.conditions: Must be directly related to business operations. Personal financial planning is not deductible.

items.name: Payroll Service Fees
items.description: Deduct costs of payroll processing services like ADP, Gusto, or QuickBooks Payroll.
items.conditions: Must have employees or pay yourself as W-2 employee (S-corp).

items.name: Business Licensing & Permits
items.description: Deduct fees for required business licenses, permits, and regulatory filings.
items.conditions: Must be required for business operation. Annual renewal fees are deductible.

items.name: Entity Formation Costs
items.description: Deduct up to $5,000 in organizational costs for forming a new business entity.
items.conditions: First $5,000 deductible in first year if total costs under $50,000. Excess amortized over 180 months.

items.name: Registered Agent Fees
items.description: Deduct annual fees paid to a registered agent service for your business entity.
items.conditions: Must be a required business expense for maintaining entity status.

items.name: Patent & Trademark Fees
items.description: Deduct or amortize costs of obtaining patents and trademarks.
items.conditions: Legal fees can be expensed. Filing fees and costs amortized over 15 years.

items.name: Continuing Education
items.description: Deduct costs of courses, seminars, and workshops that maintain or improve job skills.
items.conditions: Must maintain or improve skills in current trade. Cannot qualify you for a new profession.

items.name: Professional Certifications
items.description: Deduct costs of obtaining and maintaining professional certifications.
items.conditions: Must be related to current profession. Initial licensing for a new career is not deductible.

items.name: Work-Related Books & Publications
items.description: Deduct cost of professional books, journals, and trade publications.
items.conditions: Must be directly related to your business or profession.

items.name: Student Loan Interest Deduction
items.description: Deduct up to $2,500 in student loan interest paid during the year.
items.conditions: Above-the-line deduction. MAGI phase-out: $80,000-$95,000 single, $165,000-$195,000 MFJ.

items.name: Lifetime Learning Credit
items.description: Tax credit of 20% of up to $10,000 in qualified education expenses ($2,000 max credit).
items.conditions: MAGI phase-out: $80,000-$90,000 single, $160,000-$180,000 MFJ. Per return, not per student.

items.name: American Opportunity Tax Credit
items.description: Credit up to $2,500 per student for first 4 years of post-secondary education. 40% refundable.
items.conditions: Student must be enrolled at least half-time. MAGI phase-out: $80,000-$90,000 single, $160,000-$180,000 MFJ.

items.name: Online Course Subscriptions
items.description: Deduct subscriptions to business-related online learning platforms.
items.conditions: Must be related to current trade or business. Keep records linking courses to business.

items.name: Conference & Seminar Registration
items.description: Deduct registration fees for business conferences, conventions, and seminars.
items.conditions: Must be related to your trade or business. Travel to conference may also be deductible.

items.name: Professional Membership Dues
items.description: Deduct dues for professional organizations and trade associations.
items.conditions: Must be related to your profession. Lobbying-related portions are not deductible.

items.name: Educator Expense Deduction
items.description: K-12 teachers can deduct up to $300 for unreimbursed classroom supplies.
items.conditions: Must be K-12 teacher, instructor, counselor, or principal. At least 900 hours of work.

items.name: Self-Employed Health Insurance Deduction
items.description: Deduct 100% of health insurance premiums for yourself, spouse, and dependents.
items.conditions: Cannot exceed net self-employment income. Not eligible if you can participate in employer plan.

items.name: General Liability Insurance
items.description: Deduct premiums for business general liability insurance.
items.conditions: Must be ordinary and necessary business expense.

items.name: Professional Liability (E&O) Insurance
items.description: Deduct errors and omissions or malpractice insurance premiums.
items.conditions: Must be related to your professional practice.

items.name: Workers Compensation Insurance
items.description: Deduct workers compensation insurance premiums.
items.conditions: Required in most states if you have employees.

items.name: Business Property Insurance
items.description: Deduct premiums for insuring business equipment, inventory, and property.
items.conditions: Must insure business property. Home-based business may use business-use percentage.

items.name: Cyber Liability Insurance
items.description: Deduct premiums for cyber liability and data breach insurance.
items.conditions: Must be related to business operations. Increasingly common for businesses handling data.

items.name: Business Interruption Insurance
items.description: Deduct premiums for business interruption or business continuity insurance.
items.conditions: Ordinary and necessary business expense.

items.name: Key Person Life Insurance
items.description: Premiums for key person insurance are deductible if the business is not the beneficiary.
items.conditions: Premiums are NOT deductible if business is the beneficiary. Deductible if part of compensation.

items.name: Long-Term Care Insurance (Self-Employed)
items.description: Self-employed can deduct age-based portion of long-term care insurance premiums.
items.conditions: Deduction limited by age-based caps. Part of self-employed health insurance deduction.

items.name: Disability Insurance (Business)
items.description: Deduct business overhead disability insurance premiums.
items.conditions: Business overhead expense policies are deductible. Personal disability premiums are not.

items.name: 401(k) Employee Contributions
items.description: Defer up to $23,500 in employee contributions to a 401(k) plan in 2025.
items.conditions: Additional $7,500 catch-up for age 50+. New super catch-up of $11,250 for ages 60-63.

items.name: 401(k) Employer Match/Profit Sharing
items.description: Employer contributions to 401(k) plan, deductible by the business.
items.conditions: Total employee + employer contributions cannot exceed $70,000 in 2025 (or $77,500 with catch-up).

items.name: Solo 401(k) Contributions
items.description: Self-employed can contribute as both employee ($23,500) and employer (25% of net SE income).
items.conditions: No employees other than spouse. Total limit $70,000 in 2025 ($77,500 with catch-up).

items.name: SEP IRA Contributions
items.description: Contribute up to 25% of net self-employment income, max $70,000 in 2025.
items.conditions: Must contribute same percentage for all eligible employees. Can be funded until tax filing deadline.

items.name: SIMPLE IRA Contributions
items.description: Employee contributions up to $16,500 in 2025, plus employer match or 2% nonelective.
items.conditions: Catch-up of $3,500 for age 50+. Business must have 100 or fewer employees.

items.name: Traditional IRA Contribution
items.description: Contribute up to $7,000 to a traditional IRA ($8,000 if 50+) for potential tax deduction.
items.conditions: Deductibility depends on participation in employer plan and income. MAGI phase-outs apply.

items.name: Defined Benefit Plan Contributions
items.description: Contribute substantial amounts to a defined benefit pension plan, actuarially determined.
items.conditions: Annual benefit limited to $280,000. Requires actuary. Best for high-income, older self-employed.

items.name: Retirement Plan Setup Credit
items.description: Tax credit up to $5,000 per year for 3 years for small employers starting a retirement plan.
items.conditions: 100 or fewer employees receiving $5,000+ compensation. Covers 100% of startup costs up to $5,000.

items.name: Saver's Credit (Retirement Savings Contribution Credit)
items.description: Credit of 10-50% of retirement contributions, up to $1,000 ($2,000 MFJ).
items.conditions: AGI limits: $38,250 single, $57,375 HOH, $76,500 MFJ. Must be 18+, not a student or dependent.

items.name: Investment Advisory Fees (Business)
items.description: Deduct investment management fees related to business investments.
items.conditions: Must be related to business investment activity. Personal investment fees are not deductible 2018-2025.

items.name: HSA Contributions
items.description: Contribute up to $4,300 (self) or $8,550 (family) to a Health Savings Account in 2025.
items.conditions: Must have qualifying HDHP. Additional $1,000 catch-up for age 55+. Above-the-line deduction.

items.name: FSA Contributions (Health)
items.description: Contribute up to $3,300 pre-tax to a health Flexible Spending Account in 2025.
items.conditions: Use-it-or-lose-it with $640 rollover option. Not available with HSA (limited-purpose FSA excepted).

items.name: Medical Expenses (Above 7.5% AGI)
items.description: Deduct unreimbursed medical expenses exceeding 7.5% of AGI.
items.conditions: Must itemize deductions. Only amount exceeding 7.5% of AGI is deductible.

items.name: Prescription & Medical Supplies
items.description: Deduct prescription medications and medical supplies as part of medical expense deduction.
items.conditions: Only counts toward medical expenses exceeding 7.5% AGI threshold. Must itemize.

items.name: Dental & Vision Expenses
items.description: Deduct unreimbursed dental and vision care costs as medical expenses.
items.conditions: Included in medical expense deduction above 7.5% AGI. Must itemize.

items.name: Health Insurance Premium Tax Credit
items.description: Premium tax credit for marketplace health insurance based on household income.
items.conditions: Purchased through marketplace. Income 100-400% FPL (expanded through 2025). Cannot be offered affordable employer coverage.

items.name: Medical Travel Expenses
items.description: Deduct mileage ($0.22/mile in 2025), parking, and tolls for medical travel.
items.conditions: Part of medical expense deduction above 7.5% AGI. Keep mileage log for medical trips.

items.name: Long-Term Care Expenses
items.description: Deduct qualified long-term care services as medical expenses.
items.conditions: Must be chronically ill individual. Part of medical expense deduction above 7.5% AGI.

items.name: Dependent Care FSA
items.description: Contribute up to $5,000 pre-tax for dependent care expenses.
items.conditions: $5,000 limit for MFJ, $2,500 for MFS. Cannot also claim full dependent care credit on same expenses.

items.name: COBRA Premium Assistance
items.description: Employer-paid COBRA premiums may be tax-deductible as a business expense.
items.conditions: Employer-paid premiums are deductible as compensation expense.

items.name: Computer Peripherals & Accessories
items.description: Deduct keyboards, mice, webcams, headsets, and other peripherals for business.
items.conditions: Must be used for business. Items under $200 typically expensed immediately.

items.name: Software Subscriptions
items.description: Deduct SaaS subscriptions, cloud services, and software licenses used for business.
items.conditions: Must be used for business. Subscription fees are deducted in the year paid.

items.name: Printer Ink & Paper
items.description: Deduct printer supplies including ink, toner, and paper for business use.
items.conditions: Ordinary and necessary business supplies.

items.name: Stationery & Business Cards
items.description: Deduct letterhead, envelopes, business cards, and branded stationery.
items.conditions: Must be used for business purposes.

items.name: Postage & Shipping
items.description: Deduct postage, shipping costs, and courier services for business.
items.conditions: Must be for business-related shipments.

items.name: Cloud Storage & Backup Services
items.description: Deduct cloud storage subscriptions (Dropbox, Google Drive, AWS) for business.
items.conditions: Business-use portion is deductible. Allocate if shared with personal use.

items.name: Domain Names & Hosting
items.description: Deduct domain registration, web hosting, and DNS service fees.
items.conditions: Must be for business websites and services.

items.name: Project Management & Productivity Tools
items.description: Deduct subscriptions to tools like Slack, Asana, Notion, Trello for business.
items.conditions: Must be used for business operations.

items.name: Accounting Software
items.description: Deduct QuickBooks, FreshBooks, Xero, and similar accounting software subscriptions.
items.conditions: Must be used for business bookkeeping and accounting.

items.name: Office Cleaning Supplies
items.description: Deduct cleaning supplies and janitorial products for business premises.
items.conditions: Must be for business workspace. Home office requires business-use allocation.

items.name: Website Design & Development
items.description: Deduct costs of building or redesigning a business website.
items.conditions: Ongoing maintenance is expensed. Initial build costs may need to be amortized.

items.name: Social Media Advertising
items.description: Deduct Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, and other social media ad spending.
items.conditions: Must be for business promotion. Keep records of campaigns and business purpose.

items.name: Google Ads & PPC Advertising
items.description: Deduct pay-per-click advertising costs on Google, Bing, and other search engines.
items.conditions: Must be for business advertising purposes.

items.name: Print Advertising
items.description: Deduct newspaper, magazine, flyer, and brochure advertising costs.
items.conditions: Must be business advertising. Political advertising is not deductible.

items.name: Promotional Materials & Swag
items.description: Deduct branded promotional items, giveaways, and marketing materials.
items.conditions: Must have business name/logo. Items over $4 must be in category of signs, display racks, or promotional materials.

items.name: SEO & Content Marketing
items.description: Deduct costs of search engine optimization, content creation, and copywriting services.
items.conditions: Must be for business promotion and marketing.

items.name: Email Marketing Services
items.description: Deduct Mailchimp, ConvertKit, and other email marketing platform subscriptions.
items.conditions: Must be for business email marketing purposes.

items.name: Photography & Videography
items.description: Deduct professional photography and videography for business marketing.
items.conditions: Must be for business promotional use.

items.name: Sponsorships & Event Marketing
items.description: Deduct costs of sponsoring events, teams, or organizations for business exposure.
items.conditions: Must have legitimate business advertising purpose. Charitable sponsorships may be charitable contributions instead.

items.name: CRM Software
items.description: Deduct customer relationship management software subscriptions (Salesforce, HubSpot).
items.conditions: Must be used for business purposes.

items.name: Business Airfare
items.description: Deduct airfare for business travel away from your tax home.
items.conditions: Must be primarily for business. International trips require allocation if mixed purpose.

items.name: Hotel & Lodging
items.description: Deduct hotel and lodging costs while traveling for business.
items.conditions: Must be away from tax home overnight. Only business days for mixed trips.

items.name: Rental Car (Business Travel)
items.description: Deduct rental car costs while traveling for business purposes.
items.conditions: Must be for business travel. Personal use days must be excluded.

items.name: Business Meals (50% Deduction)
items.description: Deduct 50% of meal costs when dining with clients, prospects, or during business travel.
items.conditions: Must have business discussion. Document who, where, business purpose. Not lavish or extravagant.

items.name: Per Diem Meals (Travel)
items.description: Use IRS per diem rates instead of tracking actual meal costs during business travel.
items.conditions: Must be traveling away from tax home. 50% deduction rate. Rates vary by location.

items.name: Conference Registration & Fees
items.description: Deduct registration fees for business conferences during travel.
items.conditions: Must be related to your business. Conference cannot be a vacation disguised as business.

items.name: Baggage & Shipping (Business Travel)
items.description: Deduct baggage fees and costs of shipping business materials during travel.
items.conditions: Must be incurred during business travel.

items.name: Travel Wi-Fi & Communication
items.description: Deduct in-flight Wi-Fi, international phone plans, and communication costs during travel.
items.conditions: Must be for business communication during business travel.

items.name: Laundry & Dry Cleaning (Extended Travel)
items.description: Deduct laundry and dry cleaning costs during extended business travel.
items.conditions: Must be during business travel away from tax home.

items.name: Tips (Business Travel)
items.description: Deduct tips paid during business travel to porters, hotel staff, etc.
items.conditions: Must be reasonable and incurred during business travel.

items.name: Employee Meals (Company Events)
items.description: Deduct 100% of meals at company-wide events, picnics, and holiday parties.
items.conditions: Must be for all employees, not select group. Company picnics and holiday parties qualify for 100%.

items.name: Office Rent
items.description: Deduct rent paid for office space, retail space, or business premises.
items.conditions: Must be used for business. Rent to related parties must be at fair market value.

items.name: Co-Working Space Membership
items.description: Deduct monthly or annual co-working space memberships and day passes.
items.conditions: Must be used for business purposes.

items.name: Storage Unit Rental
items.description: Deduct rental of storage units for business inventory, equipment, or records.
items.conditions: Must store business-related items.

items.name: Business Electricity
items.description: Deduct electricity costs for business premises.
items.conditions: 100% deductible for dedicated business space. Home office uses business-use percentage.

items.name: Business Water & Sewer
items.description: Deduct water and sewer costs for business premises.
items.conditions: 100% for dedicated business space. Pro-rated for home office.

items.name: Business Gas/Heating
items.description: Deduct gas and heating costs for business premises.
items.conditions: 100% for dedicated business space. Pro-rated for home office.

items.name: Business Internet Service
items.description: Deduct internet service for dedicated business premises.
items.conditions: 100% for business-only location. Home-based must allocate business percentage.

items.name: Trash Removal (Business)
items.description: Deduct commercial trash and waste removal for business premises.
items.conditions: Must be for business premises.

items.name: Janitorial Services
items.description: Deduct professional cleaning and janitorial services for business space.
items.conditions: Must be for business premises.

items.name: Equipment Leasing
items.description: Deduct lease payments for business equipment (copiers, machinery, etc.).
items.conditions: Operating leases are fully deductible. Capital leases treated as purchases.

items.name: Virtual Office / Mail Service
items.description: Deduct virtual office, mail forwarding, and business address services.
items.conditions: Must be used for legitimate business purposes.

items.name: MACRS Depreciation (5-Year Property)
items.description: Depreciate computers, vehicles, and office equipment over 5 years using MACRS.
items.conditions: 200% declining balance method. Half-year or mid-quarter convention applies.

items.name: MACRS Depreciation (7-Year Property)
items.description: Depreciate furniture, fixtures, and certain machinery over 7 years using MACRS.
items.conditions: 200% declining balance method. Includes office furniture and fixtures.

items.name: MACRS Depreciation (27.5-Year Residential)
items.description: Depreciate residential rental property over 27.5 years straight-line.
items.conditions: Building only (not land). Straight-line method required. Mid-month convention.

items.name: MACRS Depreciation (39-Year Commercial)
items.description: Depreciate nonresidential commercial property over 39 years straight-line.
items.conditions: Building only (not land). Straight-line method. Mid-month convention.

items.name: Section 179 Deduction (General)
items.description: Elect to expense qualifying business property up to $1,250,000 instead of depreciating.
items.conditions: Property must be placed in service during tax year. Phase-out at $3,130,000 total purchases.

items.name: Listed Property Depreciation
items.description: Special depreciation rules for property that can be used for personal purposes (vehicles, cameras).
items.conditions: Must be used more than 50% for business. If business use drops below 50%, must recapture.

items.name: Amortization of Intangibles
items.description: Amortize goodwill, patents, copyrights, and other intangibles over 15 years.
items.conditions: Applies to acquired intangible assets. Self-created intangibles have different rules.

items.name: Startup Cost Amortization
items.description: Deduct up to $5,000 in startup costs, amortize remainder over 180 months.
items.conditions: First-year deduction reduced if total startup costs exceed $50,000.

items.name: Cost Segregation (Accelerated Building Depreciation)
items.description: Reclassify building components to shorter depreciation lives through a cost segregation study.
items.conditions: Requires a qualified cost segregation study. Most beneficial for buildings over $1M.

items.name: Leasehold Improvement Depreciation
items.description: Depreciate qualified improvement property (QIP) over 15 years with bonus depreciation eligible.
items.conditions: Interior improvements to nonresidential buildings. Excludes enlargement, elevators, escalators, internal structural framework.

items.name: De Minimis Safe Harbor Election
items.description: Expense tangible property items up to $2,500 each ($5,000 with audited financials) instead of capitalizing.
items.conditions: Per-item limit. Must make annual election on tax return. $5,000 threshold requires applicable financial statement.

items.name: Cash Charitable Donations
items.description: Deduct cash donations to qualified 501(c)(3) organizations.
items.conditions: Limited to 60% of AGI for cash to public charities. Must itemize. Get written acknowledgment for $250+.

items.name: Property/Non-Cash Donations
items.description: Deduct fair market value of donated property, clothing, and household items.
items.conditions: Limited to 30% AGI for appreciated property. Appraisal required for items over $5,000. Form 8283.

items.name: Charitable Mileage Deduction
items.description: Deduct $0.14 per mile driven for charitable purposes in 2025.
items.conditions: Must keep mileage log. Set by statute, not adjusted for inflation.

items.name: Appreciated Stock Donations
items.description: Donate appreciated securities to avoid capital gains and deduct full fair market value.
items.conditions: Must hold stock over 1 year. Limited to 30% of AGI. Eliminates embedded capital gains.

items.name: Donor-Advised Fund Contribution
items.description: Contribute to a donor-advised fund for immediate deduction with future grant flexibility.
items.conditions: Deduction in year of contribution. Cash: 60% AGI limit. Appreciated property: 30% AGI.

items.name: Qualified Charitable Distribution (QCD)
items.description: Individuals 70.5+ can distribute up to $105,000 from IRA directly to charity, excluded from income.
items.conditions: Must be 70.5+. Direct from IRA to charity. Counts toward RMD. Not available from employer plans.

items.name: Volunteer Expense Reimbursement
items.description: Deduct unreimbursed out-of-pocket expenses incurred while volunteering for charity.
items.conditions: Must be directly connected to charitable service. Cannot deduct value of time or services.

items.name: Conservation Easement Donation
items.description: Deduct the value of a qualified conservation easement donated to a qualified organization.
items.conditions: Must be qualified conservation contribution. IRS closely scrutinizes syndicated easement transactions.

items.name: Charitable Deduction (Business)
items.description: C-corps can deduct charitable contributions up to 10% of taxable income. Pass-throughs deduct on personal return.
items.conditions: Pass-through entities report on K-1. Individual deduction requires itemizing.

items.name: Food Inventory Donations
items.description: Enhanced deduction for businesses donating food inventory to charitable organizations.
items.conditions: Deduction is lesser of basis plus half the gain, or twice the basis. Must be wholesome food.

items.name: State Income Tax Deduction
items.description: Deduct state and local income taxes paid, subject to SALT cap.
items.conditions: Combined SALT deduction capped at $10,000 ($5,000 MFS). Must itemize.

items.name: Property Tax Deduction
items.description: Deduct real estate property taxes, subject to SALT cap.
items.conditions: Shares the $10,000 SALT cap with state income tax. Must itemize.

items.name: SALT Cap Workaround (PTE Tax)
items.description: Many states allow pass-through entity tax elections to bypass the $10,000 SALT cap.
items.conditions: Available in 30+ states. Entity pays state tax and deducts on entity return. Members get state credit.

items.name: State Sales Tax Deduction
items.description: Deduct state sales tax instead of state income tax if more beneficial.
items.conditions: Choose sales tax OR income tax (not both). Subject to SALT cap. Use IRS tables or actual receipts.

items.name: State Tax Credits for Retirement
items.description: Some states offer credits or deductions for retirement income or contributions.
items.conditions: State-specific. Check your state tax code. Common in states without income tax exemptions.

items.name: State R&D Tax Credit
items.description: Many states offer research and development tax credits in addition to federal.
items.conditions: Must have qualifying R&D activities. State rules differ from federal. Some states have refundable credits.

items.name: State Small Business Health Insurance Credit
items.description: Some states offer additional credits for small businesses providing health insurance.
items.conditions: State-specific eligibility. Usually for small employers with fewer than 25 employees.

items.name: State Historic Preservation Credit
items.description: State-level tax credits for rehabilitating historic buildings.
items.conditions: Building must be listed or eligible for historic register. Credit percentages vary by state.

items.name: State Energy Efficiency Credits
items.description: State-level credits for energy-efficient improvements beyond federal credits.
items.conditions: State-specific. May cover solar, EV charging, insulation, windows, and HVAC.

items.name: State Opportunity Zone Benefits
items.description: Some states offer additional tax benefits for investments in designated opportunity zones.
items.conditions: Must invest in a Qualified Opportunity Fund in a designated zone. State rules may differ from federal.

items.name: Mortgage Interest Deduction
items.description: Deduct interest on mortgage debt up to $750,000 for primary and secondary residences.
items.conditions: Acquisition debt up to $750,000 ($375,000 MFS). Pre-12/15/2017 debt grandfathered at $1M. Must itemize.

items.name: Student Loan Interest Deduction
items.description: Deduct up to $2,500 of student loan interest paid as an above-the-line deduction.
items.conditions: MAGI phase-out: $80,000-$95,000 single, $165,000-$195,000 MFJ. Cannot be claimed as dependent.

items.name: Business Loan Interest
items.description: Deduct interest on loans used for business purposes.
items.conditions: Loan proceeds must be used for business. Business interest limitation may apply for large businesses.

items.name: Business Credit Card Interest
items.description: Deduct interest on credit card charges that are business expenses.
items.conditions: Must be business charges. Personal credit card interest is never deductible.

items.name: Home Equity Loan Interest (Used for Home Improvement)
items.description: Deduct interest on home equity loans if used to buy, build, or substantially improve your home.
items.conditions: Combined with first mortgage, total cannot exceed $750,000. Must be used for home improvement.

items.name: Investment Interest Expense
items.description: Deduct interest on debt used to purchase taxable investments, up to net investment income.
items.conditions: Limited to net investment income. Excess carries forward. Form 4952 required.

items.name: Margin Interest (Investment)
items.description: Deduct margin interest paid on investment accounts up to net investment income.
items.conditions: Must itemize. Limited to net investment income. Cannot use against qualified dividends or long-term gains unless elected.

items.name: Equipment Financing Interest
items.description: Deduct interest on loans for business equipment purchases.
items.conditions: Equipment must be used for business. Interest is deductible as a business expense.

items.name: Real Estate Loan Interest (Investment Property)
items.description: Deduct mortgage interest on investment/rental real estate properties.
items.conditions: No $750,000 cap for investment property. Deducted against rental income on Schedule E.

items.name: Points Paid on Mortgage
items.description: Deduct points (prepaid interest) paid when obtaining a mortgage on primary residence.
items.conditions: Purchase mortgage points deductible in year paid. Refinance points amortized over loan life.

items.name: Child Tax Credit
items.description: Credit of $2,000 per qualifying child under 17. Up to $1,700 refundable in 2025.
items.conditions: Child must be under 17, US citizen/resident. MAGI phase-out starts at $200,000 ($400,000 MFJ).

items.name: Other Dependent Credit
items.description: Credit of $500 for qualifying dependents who are not qualifying children.
items.conditions: For dependents who don't qualify for child tax credit (e.g., children 17+, elderly parents).

items.name: Child and Dependent Care Credit
items.description: Credit of 20-35% of childcare expenses, up to $3,000 (one child) or $6,000 (two+).
items.conditions: Child under 13 or disabled dependent. Both spouses must work or be in school. Form 2441.

items.name: Dependent Care FSA
items.description: Exclude up to $5,000 in dependent care expenses from taxable income.
items.conditions: $5,000 MFJ, $2,500 MFS. Reduces eligible expenses for dependent care credit.

items.name: Adoption Tax Credit
items.description: Credit up to $17,280 per child for qualified adoption expenses in 2025.
items.conditions: MAGI phase-out: $252,150-$292,150. Special needs adoption: full credit regardless of expenses.

items.name: Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC)
items.description: Refundable credit for low-to-moderate income workers. Max $7,830 with 3+ children in 2025.
items.conditions: Income limits vary by filing status and children. Investment income must be $11,600 or less.

items.name: Head of Household Filing Status
items.description: More favorable tax brackets and higher standard deduction for qualifying single parents.
items.conditions: Must be unmarried, pay over half household costs, and have qualifying person living with you.

items.name: Nanny/Au Pair Tax Benefits
items.description: Household employer can provide dependent care benefits; nanny taxes enable legal deductions.
items.conditions: Must pay household employment taxes. Enables claiming dependent care credit/FSA.

items.name: 529 Plan State Tax Deduction
items.description: Many states offer income tax deductions for 529 education savings plan contributions.
items.conditions: No federal deduction. 34 states offer deductions or credits. Amounts vary by state.

items.name: Tuition & Fees for Dependents
items.description: Education credits (AOTC, LLC) available for qualified tuition and fees paid for dependents.
items.conditions: Claim AOTC ($2,500 max) or LLC ($2,000 max) for dependent's education. Cannot claim both for same student.

items.name: Jury Duty Pay Remitted to Employer
items.description: Deduct jury duty pay you remit to your employer who paid your salary during jury service.
items.conditions: Above-the-line deduction. Only if employer paid your salary and required you to turn over jury pay.

items.name: Gambling Losses
items.description: Deduct gambling losses up to the amount of gambling winnings reported.
items.conditions: Cannot exceed gambling winnings. Must itemize. Keep detailed records of wins and losses.

items.name: Moving Expenses (Military Only)
items.description: Active-duty military can deduct unreimbursed moving expenses for permanent station change.
items.conditions: Only for active-duty military with permanent change of station orders. Above-the-line deduction.

items.name: Federal R&D Tax Credit
items.description: Credit for qualified research expenses. Small businesses can apply against payroll tax.
items.conditions: Must have qualifying research activities. Startups can offset up to $500,000 in payroll taxes.

items.name: Work Opportunity Tax Credit (WOTC)
items.description: Credit for hiring individuals from targeted groups (veterans, ex-felons, etc.).
items.conditions: Employee must be from targeted group. Max $2,400-$9,600 per employee. Must certify with state agency.

items.name: Disabled Access Credit
items.description: Credit of 50% of expenditures between $250-$10,250 for ADA accessibility improvements.
items.conditions: Small business with revenue under $1M or 30 or fewer employees. Form 8826.

items.name: Energy Efficient Home Improvement Credit
items.description: Credit of 30% for qualifying energy improvements: insulation, windows, doors, HVAC, heat pumps.
items.conditions: Annual limit $3,200. Sub-limits: $1,200 general, $2,000 heat pumps. Must be primary residence.

items.name: Residential Clean Energy Credit
items.description: Credit of 30% for solar panels, solar water heaters, geothermal, battery storage, and wind.
items.conditions: No cap for most items. Must be installed in your home. Battery storage minimum 3 kWh.

items.name: Qualified Business Income (QBI) Deduction
items.description: Deduct up to 20% of qualified business income from pass-through entities.
items.conditions: Phase-out for specified service businesses above $191,950 single / $383,900 MFJ. W-2/property limits may apply.

items.name: Self-Employment Tax Deduction (50%)
items.description: Deduct the employer-equivalent portion (50%) of self-employment tax above the line.
items.conditions: Automatic above-the-line deduction. Calculated on Schedule SE.

items.name: Bad Debt Deduction
items.description: Deduct business debts that become worthless during the tax year.
items.conditions: Must be a bona fide debt. Must be wholly or partially worthless. Document collection efforts.

items.name: Net Operating Loss (NOL) Carryforward
items.description: Carry forward net operating losses indefinitely to offset up to 80% of future taxable income.
items.conditions: 80% of taxable income limit. No carryback (except farming). Carried forward indefinitely.

items.name: Casualty & Theft Loss (Federally Declared Disaster)
items.description: Deduct losses from federally declared disasters exceeding 10% of AGI.
items.conditions: Must be in a federally declared disaster area. Reduced by $100 per event and 10% of AGI.

items.name: Alimony Payments (Pre-2019 Agreements)
items.description: Deduct alimony paid under divorce agreements executed before January 1, 2019.
items.conditions: Only for agreements finalized before 1/1/2019 not modified to adopt new rules. Above-the-line.

items.name: Foreign Tax Credit
items.description: Credit for income taxes paid to foreign governments on foreign-source income.
items.conditions: Cannot exceed US tax on foreign-source income. Form 1116. Can choose credit or deduction.

items.name: Small Employer Health Insurance Credit
items.description: Credit up to 50% of premiums paid for small businesses providing employee health insurance.
items.conditions: Fewer than 25 FTE employees. Average annual wages below $58,000. Pay at least 50% of premiums.

items.name: Employee Retention Credit Carryforward
items.description: Unused ERC from 2020-2021 may still be claimed via amended returns if eligible.
items.conditions: Must have been eligible employer during COVID period. IRS moratorium on new claims; consult advisor.

items.name: Business Vehicle Wash & Detailing
items.description: Deduct vehicle cleaning and detailing costs for business-use vehicles.
items.conditions: Only with actual expense method. Pro-rated by business use percentage.

items.name: Business Meals While Traveling
items.description: Deduct 50% of meal costs while traveling overnight for business.
items.conditions: Must be traveling away from tax home. Meals not lavish or extravagant.

items.name: Employer-Provided Childcare Credit
items.description: Credit of 25% of qualified childcare facility expenditures plus 10% of resource/referral costs.
items.conditions: Maximum credit $150,000. Must maintain qualified childcare facility or contract with qualified provider.

items.name: Business Gifts
items.description: Deduct business gifts up to $25 per recipient per year.
items.conditions: Limit is $25 per person per year. Incidental costs like engraving or gift wrapping excluded from limit.

items.name: Employee Wages & Salaries
items.description: Deduct reasonable wages, salaries, bonuses, and commissions paid to employees.
items.conditions: Must be reasonable compensation for services rendered. S-corp owner must take reasonable salary.

items.name: Employer Payroll Taxes
items.description: Deduct the employer portion of FICA (Social Security and Medicare) taxes.
items.conditions: Employer share of Social Security (6.2%) and Medicare (1.45%) on employee wages.

items.name: Employee Benefits (Non-Insurance)
items.description: Deduct cost of employee benefit programs: education assistance, transit benefits, wellness programs.
items.conditions: Must be available to employees. Some benefits have statutory exclusion limits.

items.name: Bank & Financial Fees (Business)
items.description: Deduct business bank account fees, merchant processing fees, and wire transfer charges.
items.conditions: Must be for business accounts. Personal banking fees are not deductible.

items.name: Contract Labor (1099 Workers)
items.description: Deduct payments to independent contractors for business services.
items.conditions: Issue 1099-NEC for payments of $600+. Must be legitimate independent contractor relationship.

items.name: Uniforms & Work Clothing
items.description: Deduct cost and maintenance of required uniforms or protective clothing not suitable for everyday wear.
items.conditions: Must be required for work and not suitable for everyday wear. Safety equipment qualifies.

items.name: Business Use of Personal Vehicle (Reimbursement)
items.description: S-corps and employers can reimburse employees for business mileage under an accountable plan.
items.conditions: Must be under accountable plan with substantiation. Reimbursement at IRS standard rate.

items.name: Health Reimbursement Arrangement (HRA)
items.description: Employers can set up HRAs to reimburse employees for medical expenses tax-free.
items.conditions: QSEHRA limit: $6,350 self-only, $12,800 family in 2025. ICHRA has no cap.

items.name: Qualified Small Business Stock Exclusion (Section 1202)
items.description: Exclude up to 100% of gain on qualified small business stock held 5+ years.
items.conditions: Must hold stock 5+ years. C-corp with gross assets under $50M at issuance. Exclusion up to $10M or 10x basis.

items.name: Opportunity Zone Investment Deferral
items.description: Defer and potentially reduce capital gains by investing in Qualified Opportunity Funds.
items.conditions: Must invest capital gains within 180 days. Hold 10+ years for exclusion of new gains.

items.name: Mortgage Interest Deduction (Rental Property)
items.description: Deduct mortgage interest on loans used to acquire or improve rental real estate property.
items.conditions: Interest must be on debt used to acquire, build, or improve rental property. Report on Schedule E.

items.name: Property Tax Deduction (Rental)
items.description: Deduct real estate taxes paid on rental properties as a business expense on Schedule E.
items.conditions: Rental property taxes are fully deductible as business expense (not subject to SALT cap). Report on Schedule E.

items.name: Rental Property Depreciation (Residential 27.5-Year)
items.description: Depreciate residential rental building cost over 27.5 years using straight-line method.
items.conditions: Building portion only (exclude land). Begins when placed in service. Mid-month convention applies.

items.name: Commercial Property Depreciation (39-Year)
items.description: Depreciate nonresidential real property over 39 years using straight-line depreciation.
items.conditions: Nonresidential real property. 39-year straight-line. Mid-month convention. Excludes land.

items.name: 1031 Exchange Tax Deferral
items.description: Defer capital gains tax by exchanging investment real property for like-kind property.
items.conditions: Must use qualified intermediary. 45-day identification and 180-day closing deadlines. Both properties for investment/business use.

items.name: Conservation Easement Charitable Deduction
items.description: Charitable deduction for donating a permanent conservation easement on qualified real property.
items.conditions: Qualified appraisal required. Easement must be permanent. 50% AGI limit (100% for qualified farmers/ranchers). 15-year carryforward.

items.name: Rental Repairs and Maintenance
items.description: Deduct ordinary and necessary repair expenses for rental property in the year incurred.
items.conditions: Must be repairs (not improvements). Improvements must be capitalized and depreciated. De minimis safe harbor: $2,500 per item.

items.name: Rental Insurance Premiums
items.description: Deduct insurance premiums paid on rental properties (fire, liability, flood, etc.).
items.conditions: Must be ordinary and necessary business insurance for rental activity.

items.name: Property Management Fees
items.description: Deduct fees paid to property management companies for managing rental properties.
items.conditions: Typical fee: 8-12% of rental income. Must be for legitimate management services.

items.name: Rental Travel Expenses
items.description: Deduct travel costs to manage, maintain, or collect rent from rental properties.
items.conditions: Local travel: standard mileage rate or actual expenses. Overnight: reasonable lodging and meals (50% for meals).

items.name: Depreciation Recapture (Section 1250)
items.description: Unrecaptured §1250 gain on sale of depreciable real property is taxed at a maximum 25% rate.
items.conditions: Applies on sale of real property. Recapture equals lesser of gain or accumulated depreciation. Maximum 25% tax rate.

items.name: Historic Rehabilitation Tax Credit
items.description: 20% federal tax credit for qualified rehabilitation expenses on certified historic structures.
items.conditions: Certified historic structure. Substantial rehabilitation test. Credit claimed ratably over 5 years. Must reduce depreciable basis.

items.name: Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC)
items.description: Federal tax credit for investment in qualified low-income housing projects, claimed over 10 years.
items.conditions: Allocated by state housing agencies. 15-year compliance period. Rent and income restrictions on tenants.

items.name: Installment Sale Interest Income Deferral
items.description: Spread gain recognition from property sales over the installment period, deferring tax liability.
items.conditions: At least one payment after tax year of sale. Depreciation recapture recognized in year of sale. Interest charges above $5M.

items.name: Qualified Land Conservation Contribution
items.description: Enhanced deduction for qualified farmers and ranchers donating conservation easements — up to 100% of AGI.
items.conditions: Qualified farmer/rancher. Land must remain available for farming. 100% AGI limit. 15-year carryforward.

strategies.name: Entity Structure Optimization
strategies.description: Choosing the right business entity (LLC, S-Corp, C-Corp) can save thousands in self-employment and income taxes. S-Corps allow owners to split income between salary and distributions, reducing SE tax.
strategies.requirements: Consult with CPA; may require state filings and additional tax returns
strategies.example: A freelancer earning $150K switches to S-Corp, pays $80K salary and takes $70K as distribution, saving ~$10,700 in SE tax.
strategies.caveats: S-Corp salary must be 'reasonable compensation'. IRS scrutinizes low salaries. Additional payroll costs.

strategies.name: Home Office + Vehicle Combo
strategies.description: Combining home office and vehicle deductions maximizes savings. Your home office establishes your 'tax home,' making all trips to client sites deductible business mileage.
strategies.requirements: Dedicated home office space; vehicle used for business; mileage log
strategies.example: With a qualifying home office, your 30-mile daily commute to a client becomes 100% deductible business mileage at $0.70/mile.
strategies.caveats: Home office must meet exclusive use test. Keep contemporaneous mileage records.

strategies.name: Retirement Contribution Maximization
strategies.description: Maximize pre-tax retirement contributions across multiple account types. Self-employed individuals can contribute to both a SEP IRA and a Solo 401(k) for different businesses.
strategies.requirements: Earned income; age-based limits apply; contribution deadlines vary by plan type
strategies.example: A self-employed person maxes Solo 401(k) at $23,500 employee + 25% employer match on net SE income, deferring $40K+ in taxable income.
strategies.caveats: Contribution limits are aggregate across employers for 401(k). SEP IRA and Solo 401(k) cannot both be used for same business.

strategies.name: Income Shifting via S-Corp
strategies.description: S-Corp election allows business owners to reduce self-employment tax by paying themselves a reasonable salary and taking remaining profits as distributions not subject to SE tax.
strategies.requirements: S-Corp election filed with IRS; payroll system; reasonable salary determination
strategies.example: Owner earns $200K, pays $100K salary (15.3% SE tax) and takes $100K as distribution (0% SE tax), saving ~$15,300.
strategies.caveats: Must pay reasonable compensation. IRS can reclassify distributions as wages. Requires payroll tax filings.

strategies.name: Section 199A QBI Deduction
strategies.description: Qualified Business Income deduction allows pass-through business owners to deduct up to 20% of qualified business income from their taxable income.
strategies.requirements: Pass-through business income; income below thresholds for specified service trades
strategies.example: A consultant with $150K QBI deducts 20% = $30,000 from taxable income, saving $7,200 at 24% bracket.
strategies.caveats: Specified service trades (law, accounting, health, consulting) face income phase-outs. W-2 wage and capital limits apply above thresholds.

strategies.name: Augusta Rule (Section 280A)
strategies.description: Rent your home to your business for up to 14 days per year tax-free. The business deducts the rent, and you don't report the rental income on your personal return.
strategies.requirements: Must have a legitimate business entity separate from you; fair market rent; documented business purpose for each rental day
strategies.example: S-Corp rents owner's home for 12 board meetings at $1,500/day = $18,000 deduction for the corp, tax-free income for the owner.
strategies.caveats: Must charge fair market value. Document each business use day. IRS scrutinizes excessive amounts. Get a rental comparable study.

strategies.name: Hire Your Children
strategies.description: Employ your children under 18 in your sole proprietorship. Wages are deductible to the business and may be tax-free to the child up to the standard deduction amount.
strategies.requirements: Child must perform legitimate work; age-appropriate tasks; reasonable pay for work performed
strategies.example: Pay your 15-year-old $14,600 for office work. Fully deductible to you, zero tax to child (under standard deduction), no FICA if sole prop.
strategies.caveats: Only works for sole proprietorships and husband-wife partnerships for FICA exemption. Must be real work at reasonable rates. Keep time records.

strategies.name: Tax Loss Harvesting
strategies.description: Sell investments at a loss to offset capital gains and up to $3,000 of ordinary income. Immediately reinvest in similar (not substantially identical) assets.
strategies.requirements: Taxable investment account; unrealized losses; understanding of wash sale rules
strategies.example: Sell stock with $15K loss, offset $12K in capital gains and deduct $3K against income. Reinvest in similar ETF immediately.
strategies.caveats: Wash sale rule: cannot buy substantially identical security within 30 days before or after. Losses carry forward indefinitely.
strategies.irs_reference: IRC §1091 (wash sale); IRC §1211; Schedule D; Form 8949

strategies.name: Backdoor Roth IRA
strategies.description: High earners can contribute to a Roth IRA indirectly by making a non-deductible traditional IRA contribution and immediately converting it. Provides tax-free growth.
strategies.requirements: No existing pre-tax IRA balances (to avoid pro-rata rule); income above Roth contribution limits
strategies.example: Contribute $7,000 non-deductible to traditional IRA, convert to Roth next day. Pay minimal tax on any gains during conversion.
strategies.caveats: Pro-rata rule makes this expensive if you have existing traditional IRA balances. Consider rolling pre-tax IRAs into 401(k) first.

strategies.name: Cost Segregation Study
strategies.description: Accelerate depreciation on commercial or rental property by reclassifying building components into shorter-lived asset categories (5, 7, or 15 years vs 27.5/39 years).
strategies.requirements: Own commercial or residential rental property; hire qualified cost segregation firm; property typically valued over $500K
strategies.example: On a $1M commercial building, cost seg reclassifies $300K to 5/7/15-year property, generating $80K+ in first-year bonus depreciation.
strategies.caveats: Can trigger depreciation recapture on sale. Study costs $5K-$15K. Most beneficial for properties held long-term. Consult tax advisor.
strategies.irs_reference: IRC §168; IRS Audit Technique Guide (Cost Segregation); Form 4562

strategies.name: Real Estate Professional Status
strategies.description: Qualifying as a real estate professional allows you to deduct rental losses against ordinary income without the $25,000 passive activity loss limitation.
strategies.requirements: 750+ hours in real estate activities; more than half of working time in real estate; material participation in each rental
strategies.example: RE professional with $80K in rental depreciation losses can deduct all of it against W-2 or business income instead of carrying forward.
strategies.caveats: IRS heavily audits RE professional status. Keep detailed contemporaneous time logs. Spouse's time doesn't count toward your hours.

strategies.name: HSA Triple Tax Advantage
strategies.description: Health Savings Accounts offer a triple tax benefit: contributions are tax-deductible, growth is tax-free, and qualified medical withdrawals are tax-free.
strategies.requirements: Enrolled in a High Deductible Health Plan (HDHP); not enrolled in Medicare; not claimed as dependent
strategies.example: Family contributes max $8,550 to HSA, saves ~$2,500 in taxes at 30% bracket. Invests and lets it grow tax-free for decades.
strategies.caveats: Must have HDHP. Over-contributions face 6% penalty. Keep receipts for medical expenses indefinitely for future reimbursement.

strategies.name: Charitable Giving with Appreciated Stock
strategies.description: Donate appreciated stock held over 1 year directly to charity. Deduct the full market value and avoid capital gains tax on the appreciation entirely.
strategies.requirements: Stock held over 1 year; donated to qualified 501(c)(3); deduction limited to 30% of AGI
strategies.example: Donate $10K of stock with $3K cost basis. Deduct $10K and avoid $1,400 in capital gains tax (20% rate). Total benefit: $3,900 at 25% bracket.
strategies.caveats: 30% AGI limit (vs 60% for cash). Must be held over 1 year. Get qualified appraisal for non-publicly-traded property over $5K.

strategies.name: Donor-Advised Fund Bunching
strategies.description: Bunch multiple years of charitable donations into a single year using a Donor-Advised Fund to exceed the standard deduction, then take standard deduction in off years.
strategies.requirements: Charitable giving history; itemizable in bunching years; DAF account
strategies.example: Instead of $5K/year for 3 years, donate $15K in year 1 to DAF (itemize), take standard deduction years 2-3. Net tax savings ~$1,500.
strategies.caveats: DAF contributions are irrevocable. You advise on grants but don't control the fund. Some DAFs have minimums and fees.

strategies.name: Section 179 First-Year Expensing
strategies.description: Deduct the full cost of qualifying business equipment and software in the year of purchase instead of depreciating over multiple years. 2025 limit is $1,250,000.
strategies.requirements: Tangible personal property used >50% for business; purchased (not leased) and placed in service during tax year
strategies.example: Buy $30K in equipment. Section 179 lets you deduct all $30K this year instead of $6K/year over 5 years. Tax savings: $7,200 at 24%.
strategies.caveats: Phase-out begins at $3,130,000 in total purchases. Cannot create or increase a net operating loss. SUV limit of $30,500.

strategies.name: Accountable Plan for Employee Expenses
strategies.description: S-Corp or C-Corp sets up an accountable plan to reimburse employee-shareholders for business expenses. Reimbursements are tax-free to the employee and deductible by the corp.
strategies.requirements: Written accountable plan; business connection; adequate accounting; return of excess amounts
strategies.example: S-Corp reimburses owner for $8K in home office, cell phone, internet, and mileage. Tax-free to owner, deductible to corp.
strategies.caveats: Must have written plan. Employee must substantiate expenses within 60 days and return excess within 120 days.

strategies.name: Maximize Education Credits
strategies.description: The American Opportunity Credit ($2,500) and Lifetime Learning Credit ($2,000) provide dollar-for-dollar tax reduction for education expenses.
strategies.requirements: Enrolled in eligible educational institution; income within limits; Form 1098-T
strategies.example: Pay $4K tuition for dependent. Claim $2,500 American Opportunity Credit (40% refundable = $1,000 even with no tax liability).
strategies.caveats: AOC limited to first 4 years of post-secondary. LLC is non-refundable. Income phase-outs apply. Cannot claim both for same student.

strategies.name: Qualified Business Income Stacking
strategies.description: Structure multiple businesses or income streams to maximize the Section 199A QBI deduction across different entities and income types.
strategies.requirements: Multiple business activities or income streams; understanding of aggregation rules
strategies.example: Separate consulting (SSTB) from product sales (non-SSTB) into different entities. Non-SSTB business gets full 199A deduction regardless of income.
strategies.caveats: Aggregation rules are complex. Anti-abuse rules prevent gaming. Specified service trade limitations still apply per activity.

strategies.name: Strategic Estimated Tax Payments
strategies.description: Time your Q4 state estimated tax payment in December vs January to control which year you claim the SALT deduction, maximizing the $10,000 cap benefit.
strategies.requirements: State income tax liability; itemizing deductions; monitoring SALT cap usage
strategies.example: If you're under the $10K SALT cap, pay Q4 state estimate in December to claim it this year. If at the cap, defer to January.
strategies.caveats: $10,000 SALT cap limits benefit. Don't prepay state tax if already at cap. AMT implications possible.

strategies.name: Net Operating Loss Carryforward
strategies.description: Business losses exceeding income create a Net Operating Loss that can be carried forward indefinitely to offset up to 80% of taxable income in future years.
strategies.requirements: Business loss exceeding other income; proper NOL computation; track carryforward amounts
strategies.example: Year 1: $50K business loss, $30K W-2 income. $20K NOL carries forward. Year 2: Apply $20K NOL against income, saving $4,800 at 24%.
strategies.caveats: 80% of taxable income limit for post-2017 NOLs. No carryback allowed (except farming). Track NOL schedule carefully.

strategies.name: Health Insurance Premium Deduction (Self-Employed)
strategies.description: Self-employed individuals can deduct 100% of health, dental, and long-term care insurance premiums as an above-the-line deduction, reducing AGI.
strategies.requirements: Self-employed with net profit; not eligible for employer-sponsored plan through spouse
strategies.example: Self-employed person pays $12,000/year for family health insurance. Full above-the-line deduction saves $2,880 at 24% bracket.
strategies.caveats: Cannot exceed net self-employment income. Cannot deduct if eligible for employer plan (including spouse's). Reduces AGI but not SE tax.

strategies.name: Bonus Depreciation on Vehicles
strategies.description: Heavy SUVs and trucks (GVWR over 6,000 lbs) qualify for bonus depreciation up to $30,500 in the first year under Section 179, plus additional bonus depreciation.
strategies.requirements: Vehicle GVWR over 6,000 lbs; used >50% for business; purchased new or used; placed in service during tax year
strategies.example: Buy $65K SUV (6,100 lb GVWR) used 80% for business. Section 179: $30,500 × 80% = $24,400 first-year deduction.
strategies.caveats: Must be used >50% for business. $30,500 Section 179 limit for SUVs. Luxury auto limits apply to lighter vehicles. Keep mileage log.

strategies.name: Qualified Opportunity Zone Investment
strategies.description: Invest capital gains into Qualified Opportunity Zone Funds to defer and potentially reduce capital gains taxes, with tax-free growth if held 10+ years.
strategies.requirements: Realized capital gains; investment in certified QOZ Fund within 180 days; long-term hold commitment
strategies.example: Invest $100K capital gain into QOZ Fund. Defer gain until 2026. Hold 10+ years and pay zero tax on QOZ Fund appreciation.
strategies.caveats: Original gain deferral ends in 2026 regardless. No more basis step-up for 5/7 year holds (expired 2026). High risk real estate investments.

strategies.name: Roth Conversion Ladder
strategies.description: In low-income years, strategically convert traditional IRA/401(k) funds to Roth, paying tax at a lower bracket now to avoid higher taxes later or in RMD years.
strategies.requirements: Traditional IRA or 401(k) balance; low-income year (sabbatical, retirement, business downturn)
strategies.example: In a year earning $40K (12% bracket), convert $50K from traditional to Roth. Pay $6K tax now instead of $12K+ at 24% in retirement.
strategies.caveats: Conversions are taxable income. Can push you into higher brackets. Medicare IRMAA surcharges if over thresholds. No undo (recharacterization).

strategies.name: Business Meal Deduction Strategy
strategies.description: Business meals with clients, prospects, or business associates are 50% deductible. Proper documentation turns everyday meals into legitimate deductions.
strategies.requirements: Business discussion before, during, or after meal; receipt with date, amount, attendees, business purpose
strategies.example: 20 client lunches at avg $80 = $1,600. 50% deductible = $800 deduction. At 24% bracket, saves $192.
strategies.caveats: Must have business discussion (not just goodwill). Keep receipts with who/what/when/why. Entertainment is NOT deductible (only the meal portion).

strategies.name: Home Sale Exclusion Planning
strategies.description: Exclude up to $250K ($500K married) of capital gain from the sale of your primary residence if you've lived there 2 of the last 5 years.
strategies.requirements: Owned and used as primary residence for 2 of last 5 years; haven't used exclusion in last 2 years
strategies.example: Married couple sells home with $400K gain. Entire gain excluded from taxation, saving $60K+ in capital gains tax.
strategies.caveats: Partial exclusion if moved for work, health, or unforeseen circumstances. Home office depreciation after 2009 is recaptured.

strategies.name: Maximize Dependent Credits
strategies.description: Stack child tax credit, dependent care FSA, and child/dependent care credit for maximum tax savings. Each has different rules and can often be combined.
strategies.requirements: Qualifying children under 17 (CTC) or under 13 (care credit); earned income; daycare expenses
strategies.example: 2 kids: $4,000 CTC + $5,000 dependent care FSA (saves $1,500 in tax) + remaining expenses toward care credit. Total: $5,500+ savings.
strategies.caveats: CTC phases out above $200K ($400K MFJ). Care credit reduces as income rises. FSA is use-it-or-lose-it.

strategies.name: Energy Efficiency Tax Credits
strategies.description: Residential clean energy credits cover 30% of solar panels, battery storage, heat pumps, and other energy improvements with no annual cap through 2032.
strategies.requirements: Install qualifying energy equipment in your primary or secondary residence; keep manufacturer certification
strategies.example: Install $25K solar system. 30% credit = $7,500 directly off your tax bill. Carry forward unused credit to future years.
strategies.caveats: Must be your residence (not rental). Credit is non-refundable but carries forward. Energy efficient home improvement credit capped at $3,200/year for non-solar items.

strategies.name: Maximize Moving Deductions (Military)
strategies.description: Active duty military members can deduct unreimbursed moving expenses for permanent station changes. This is one of the few remaining moving deductions.
strategies.requirements: Active duty military; permanent change of station orders; expenses not reimbursed by military
strategies.example: PCS move costs $8K, military reimburses $5K. Deduct remaining $3K above-the-line. At 22% bracket, saves $660.
strategies.caveats: Only available to active duty military. Civilian moving deduction was suspended 2018-2025 by TCJA.

strategies.name: 1031 Like-Kind Exchange
strategies.description: Defer capital gains tax by exchanging one investment or business property for another of like-kind. All real property is like-kind to other real property.
strategies.requirements: Qualified intermediary; 45-day identification window; 180-day closing deadline; both properties must be held for investment or business use
strategies.example: Sell a $500K rental property with $200K gain. Exchange into a $600K property via 1031 — defer the entire $200K gain ($40K+ in taxes).
strategies.caveats: Cannot be used for primary residences. Strict timelines (45/180 days). Boot (cash or non-like-kind property received) is taxable. Depreciation recapture deferred, not eliminated.

strategies.name: Conservation Easement Donation
strategies.description: Donate a conservation easement on land you own to a qualified organization. The easement permanently restricts development, and you receive a charitable deduction for the value of the restriction.
strategies.requirements: Land with conservation value (open space, habitat, historic); qualified appraisal; donation to qualified land trust or government; permanent restriction
strategies.example: Owner of 100-acre rural property donates development rights via conservation easement valued at $300K. Claims charitable deduction over 16 years.
strategies.caveats: IRS heavily scrutinizes syndicated conservation easements. Appraisal must meet strict standards. Deduction limited to 50% AGI (or 100% for qualified farmers/ranchers). Carryforward up to 15 years.

strategies.name: Cost Segregation for Real Estate
strategies.description: Engineering-based study that reclassifies building components into shorter depreciation periods (5, 7, or 15 years) to accelerate deductions on commercial or rental property.
strategies.requirements: Own depreciable real property; engage qualified cost segregation firm; property generally valued over $500K for cost-effectiveness
strategies.example: Cost seg on a $2M apartment building reclassifies $500K of components to 5/15-year life. Combined with bonus depreciation, generates $400K+ in first-year deductions.
strategies.caveats: Depreciation recapture (§1245/§1250) on sale. Study costs $5K-$15K. IRS Audit Technique Guide sets standards. Look-back studies available for existing properties.
strategies.irs_reference: IRC §168; IRS Audit Technique Guide (Cost Segregation); Form 4562

strategies.name: Opportunity Zone Investment (QOZ)
strategies.description: Invest capital gains into a Qualified Opportunity Fund that deploys capital in designated low-income census tracts. Defer original gains and potentially eliminate taxes on new appreciation.
strategies.requirements: Realized capital gains; investment in self-certified QOF within 180 days; fund must hold 90%+ assets in QOZ property
strategies.example: Invest $200K in capital gains into QOF that develops affordable housing in QOZ. Defer gain until 2026. After 10 years, all QOF appreciation is tax-free.
strategies.caveats: Deferred gain recognition in 2026. No longer any basis step-up for 5/7-year holds. Must file Form 8996 annually. Real estate development risk.

strategies.name: Agricultural Use Reclassification
strategies.description: Reclassify land for agricultural use to receive significantly lower property tax assessments. Many states offer ag-use valuation that reduces property taxes by 50-90%.
strategies.requirements: Land meeting state acreage minimums (typically 5-10 acres); bona fide agricultural activity (crops, livestock, timber); application to county assessor
strategies.example: 40-acre property assessed at $400K residential ($8K/year tax) is reclassified as agricultural ($40K assessment, $800/year tax) — saves $7,200/year.
strategies.caveats: State-specific rules vary widely. Rollback taxes if you change use within 5-10 years. Must maintain genuine agricultural activity. Annual reporting may be required.
strategies.irs_reference: IRC §2032A (special use valuation); state assessment statutes

strategies.name: Real Estate Professional Status (REPS)
strategies.description: Qualifying as a real estate professional allows rental activities to be treated as non-passive, enabling unlimited deduction of rental losses against other income.
strategies.requirements: 750+ hours in real estate trades/businesses; more than half of personal services in RE activities; material participation in each rental (or group election)
strategies.example: Full-time RE agent with $120K income and $80K in rental depreciation losses deducts all losses against active income, saving $19,200 at 24% bracket.
strategies.caveats: IRS heavily audits REPS claims. Must keep contemporaneous time logs. Both spouses cannot aggregate hours. Consider grouping election under §1.469-9(g).

strategies.name: Passive Activity Loss Rules
strategies.description: Understand and strategically manage passive activity losses. Active participants in rental real estate can deduct up to $25,000 in losses against non-passive income.
strategies.requirements: Active participation in rental activity; modified AGI below $150,000 for full benefit; losses from passive activities
strategies.example: Active landlord with $100K AGI and $25K rental loss deducts full $25K against W-2 income, saving $6,000 at 24% bracket.
strategies.caveats: $25K allowance phases out between $100K-$150K MAGI. Suspended losses carry forward. Released on full disposition of activity.

strategies.name: Rental Property Depreciation (27.5-Year)
strategies.description: Deduct the cost of residential rental buildings over 27.5 years using straight-line depreciation. Land is not depreciable, so allocate purchase price between building and land.
strategies.requirements: Own residential rental property placed in service; determine depreciable basis (exclude land); maintain depreciation schedule
strategies.example: Buy $300K rental (land $60K, building $240K). Annual depreciation: $240K / 27.5 = $8,727. At 24% bracket, saves $2,094/year in taxes.
strategies.caveats: Depreciation recapture taxed at 25% on sale (§1250). Land is never depreciable. Must begin depreciation when property is placed in service.

strategies.name: Section 121 Primary Residence Exclusion
strategies.description: Exclude up to $250,000 ($500,000 married filing jointly) of gain from the sale of your primary residence from capital gains tax.
strategies.requirements: Owned and used home as primary residence for 2 of last 5 years; not used exclusion in past 2 years
strategies.example: Married couple sells home for $800K (basis $350K). $450K gain, fully excluded under §121. Zero capital gains tax owed.
strategies.caveats: Partial exclusion for unforeseen circumstances (job change, health). Depreciation after May 6, 1997 is recaptured. Non-qualified use periods reduce exclusion.

strategies.name: Installment Sale Reporting
strategies.description: Spread capital gain recognition over multiple years by receiving sale proceeds in installments. Reduces the tax hit of a large gain in a single year.
strategies.requirements: At least one payment received after the tax year of sale; seller-financed note; not available for publicly traded securities
strategies.example: Sell $1M property with $400K gain on 5-year installment. Report $80K gain/year instead of $400K in year 1 — stay in lower brackets each year.
